items.reason: Volume group snapshots reaching GA is a major Kubernetes storage feature; the Wiki currently has no article covering it.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: More Drivers, New Features, and the Next Era of DRA
items.summary:
<p>Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) has fundamentally changed how platform administrators handle hardware
accelerators and specialized resources in Kubernetes. In the v1.36 release, DRA
continues to mature, bringing a wave of feature graduations, critical usability
improvements, and new capabili
items.reason: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is a core advancement for handling hardware accelerators in Kubernetes and is not yet documented.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Server-Side Sharded List and Watch
items.summary:
<p>As Kubernetes clusters grow to tens of thousands of nodes, controllers that watch
high-cardinality resources like Pods face a scaling wall. Every replica of a
horizontally scaled controller receives the full stream of events from the API
server, paying the CPU, memory, and network cost to dese
items.reason: Server‑Side Sharded List and Watch addresses scalability for large clusters – a high‑impact topic absent from the Wiki.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Declarative Validation Graduates to GA
items.summary:
<p>In Kubernetes v1.36, <strong>Declarative Validation</strong> for Kubernetes native types has reached General Availability (GA).</p>
<p>For users, this means more reliable, predictable, and better-documented APIs. By moving to a declarative model, the project also unlocks the future
items.reason: Declarative Validation reaching GA improves API reliability; a dedicated Wiki page is needed.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.36: Admission Policies That Can't Be Deleted
items.summary:
<p>If you've ever tried to enforce a security policy across a fleet of
Kubernetes clusters, you've probably run into a frustrating chicken-and-egg
problem. Your admission policies are API objects, which means they don't
exist until someone creates them, and they can be deleted by anyone with
the
items.reason: Admission policies that cannot be deleted introduce important security semantics for Kubernetes clusters.

items.reason: Docker’s sandboxing approaches are relevant to container security and isolation, a topic not covered yet.

items.title: Generate Images Locally with Docker Model Runner and Open WebUI
items.summary: We've all been there: you need to generate a few images for a project, you fire up an AI image service, and suddenly you're wondering what happens to your prompts, how many credits you have left, or why that "safe content" filter rejected your perfectly reasonable request for a dragon wearing a busi
items.reason: Local AI image generation with Docker Model Runner is a new use‑case that merits a brief Wiki entry.

items.title: Precision Container Security with Docker and Black Duck
items.summary: The complexity of modern containerized applications often leaves developers drowning in a sea of "noise"—vulnerabilities that exist in the file system but pose zero actual risk to the application. The integration between Black Duck and Docker Hardened Images (DHI) provides a definitive answer to thi
items.reason: Integration of Black Duck with Docker Hardened Images provides a concrete security solution worth documenting.

items.title: A Virtual Agent team at Docker: How the Coding Agent Sandboxes team uses a fleet of agents to ship faster
items.summary: I work on Coding Agent Sandboxes, aka “sbx” at Docker. The project provides secure, microVM-based isolation for running AI coding agents like Claude Code, Gemini, Codex, Docker Agent and Kiro. Agents get full autonomy inside a sandbox (their own Docker daemon, network, filesystem) without touching y
items.reason: The Virtual Agent sandbox team showcases microVM‑based isolation for AI agents – a novel Docker feature.

items.title: From Security Blocked to Prod Ready: ClickHouse on Docker Hardened Images
items.summary: In November 2025, a team self-hosting Langfuse, an open-source LLM observability platform, on Kubernetes uploaded their ClickHouse image to AWS ECR as part of their production preparation. They found that the pipeline scanner had returned three critical vulnerabilities - not in ClickHouse, but in th
items.reason: Specific case study of ClickHouse on Docker Hardened Images is useful but too narrow to justify a separate Wiki page at this time.

items.summary:
Youth safety requirements are moving down the tech stack to operating systems and app stores—raising new questions for open source developers.

items.summary:
Researchers share in an interview how they used GitHub data to predict GDP, inequality, and emissions in ways that traditional economic data misses, along with our Q4 2025 data release.
The post How researchers are using GitHub Innovation Graph data to reveal the “digital complexity” of nations appe
items.reason: Using GitHub Innovation Graph data for socio‑economic research is a notable application that deserves coverage.

items.title: Improving token efficiency in GitHub Agentic Workflows
items.summary:
Agentic workflows that run on every pull request can quietly accumulate large API bills. Here's how we instrumented our own production workflows, found the inefficiencies, and built agents to fix them.
The post Improving token efficiency in GitHub Agentic Workflows appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Improving token efficiency in GitHub Agentic Workflows is a practical optimization topic for CI/CD pipelines.

items.title: Agent pull requests are everywhere. Here’s how to review them.

The post Agent pull requests are everywhere. Here’s how to review them. appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Guidance on reviewing AI‑generated pull requests addresses emerging workflow challenges.
